name: Cueva de los Pilares and Cerro Morrón Loop
name_original: Cueva de los Pilares y Pico Morrón — circular por el barranco de Horcajuelo
description: Hike a difficult 9.7-mile circular route to Cueva de los Pilares and Pico Morrón, gaining 2909 feet with panoramic views.
region_intro: Hiking around Añón De Moncayo offers access to the Moncayo Natural Park, a region characterized by the towering Pico Moncayo, the highest peak in the Iberian System. The landscape features diverse forest ecosystems, including extensive oak, beech, and pine groves, alongside distinctive glacial cirques, rivers, and ravines. This varied terrain provides a range of environments for outdoor exploration, from lower Mediterranean flora to alpine conditions near the summit. Best hiking trails around Añón De Moncayo The most popular hiking route is Cueva de los Pilares and Cerro Morrón Loop, a 9.7 miles (15.7 km) trail that takes 5 hours 28 minutes to complete. This difficult route involves significant elevation gain through varied mountain terrain. Another top favourite among local hikers is Refugio Valdemanzano and Barranco de la Morca - circular from the Electric Central, a moderate 6.7 miles (10.8 km) path. This route explores varied landscapes including a ravine and offers views of the Moncayo massif. Local hikers also love the Cuevas de Añón loop from Alcalá de Moncayo, a 5.4 miles (8.8 km) trail leading through the Cuevas de Añón area, often completed in about 2 hours 23 minutes. Hiking around Añón De Moncayo is defined by diverse forest ecosystems, glacial cirques, and picturesque ravines. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate loops to more challenging ascents. The routes in Añón De Moncayo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 100 reviews. More than 1000 hikers have used komoot to explore Añón De Moncayo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available around Añón De Moncayo?
A: There are 11 hiking routes available around Añón De Moncayo, offering a variety of experiences within the Moncayo Natural Park. These trails range from moderate loops to more challenging ascents, catering to different skill levels.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect to see while hiking in Añón De Moncayo?
A: Hiking in Añón De Moncayo offers a rich tapestry of landscapes. You'll encounter diverse forest ecosystems, including extensive oak, beech, holm oak, and pine groves. The terrain also features distinctive glacial cirques, picturesque rivers, and ravines, with vegetation ranging from Mediterranean species at lower altitudes to alpine flora near the summit of Pico Moncayo.

Q: Are there any easy or family-friendly hiking options in the area?
A: Yes, while many routes offer moderate challenges, there are easier options. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/17544360" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cuevas de Añón loop from Alcalá de Moncayo&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 5.4-mile (8.8 km) trail that explores the charming Cuevas de Añón area, suitable for a less strenuous outing. The Moncayo Natural Park Visitor Center in Añón de Moncayo can provide further details on family-friendly trails.

Q: What are some notable viewpoints or natural attractions to look out for?
A: The region is rich in natural attractions. You can find breathtaking vistas from the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5304172" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Moncayo&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5291398" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cabezo de la Mata&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5288045" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cueva de los Pilares&lt;/a&gt; is a fascinating cave to explore. Additionally, the Balcón del Huecha offers panoramic views of the Moncayo massif and the Huecha valley, and the Cuevas de Añón area itself is a charming spot with natural springs and picnic areas.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in Añón De Moncayo?
A: The hiking routes in Añón De Moncayo are highly regarded by the komoot community, with an average rating of 4.7 stars from over 125 reviews. Hikers often praise the diverse forest ecosystems, the well-maintained trails, and the impressive views, especially towards the Moncayo massif.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes available?
A: Yes, several circular routes are popular in the area. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e971994351/cueva-de-los-pilares-and-cerro-morron-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cueva de los Pilares and Cerro Morrón Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a challenging 9.7-mile (15.7 km) option. For a moderate experience, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e972105181/refugio-valdemanzano-and-barranco-de-la-morca-circular-from-the-electric-central" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Refugio Valdemanzano and Barranco de la Morca - circular from the Electric Central&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24312335" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Añón de Moncayo – Cuevas de Añón loop from Añón de Moncayo&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Añón De Moncayo?
A: The Moncayo Natural Park offers hiking opportunities throughout the year, but conditions vary. Spring and autumn generally provide pleasant temperatures and vibrant scenery, with autumn showcasing beautiful fall foliage in the diverse forests. Summer can be warm, especially at lower altitudes, while winter hiking, particularly at higher elevations like Pico Moncayo, requires appropriate gear due to snow and colder conditions.

Q: Is it possible to hike to Pico Moncayo from Añón De Moncayo?
A: Añón de Moncayo serves as a key starting point for exploring the Moncayo Natural Park, which includes routes leading towards Pico Moncayo (Pico San Miguel), the highest peak in the Iberian System. Ascents to the summit reward hikers with impressive panoramic views. These routes typically traverse beautiful oak forests and involve significant elevation gain, making them challenging.

Q: Are dogs allowed on the hiking trails in Moncayo Natural Park?
A: The Moncayo Natural Park generally welcomes dogs on its trails, but it's always recommended to keep them on a leash to protect wildlife and ensure the safety of other hikers. Please be mindful of local regulations and ensure your dog is well-behaved and doesn't disturb the natural environment.

Q: Where can I find parking for the trailheads in Añón De Moncayo?
A: Parking is typically available near the Moncayo Natural Park Visitor Center in Añón de Moncayo, which serves as a common starting point for many trails. Additionally, specific trailheads, such as those for the Cuevas de Añón area or the Electric Central, may have designated parking spots. It's advisable to check specific route details for precise parking information.

Q: Are there any trails that feature the Morana Ravine or Horcajuelo Meadows?
A: Yes, the PR-Z 3 trail, a local route within the Moncayo Natural Park, provides access to the scenic Morana Ravine and Horcajuelo Meadows. These natural wonders offer picturesque routes for hikers looking to explore more of the park's diverse terrain.
tips: Spectacular setting in the Moncayo Natural Park with these formations at the entrance to the Cueva de los Pilares, at the foot of the Muela del Col

Located at an altitude of 1,731 meters, is the top of Cerro Morrón, located in the Sierra de la Muela and within the Moncayo Natural Park with impressive views. Let's not forget to visit the Cueva de los Pilares that are close to us!

From this point we will have spectacular views of the Peñas de Herrera that will accompany us for a good part of the way if we go to Cerro Morrón or to the Peñas de Herrera themselves, both located in the Sierra del Moncayo

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike this route?
faqs.answer: While the trail can be hiked in various seasons, spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant conditions with moderate temperatures and vibrant scenery. On clear days, you'll get the best panoramic views from the Cerro Morrón Summit, potentially even seeing the Pyrenees. Winter hiking is possible but requires appropriate gear and experience due to potentially challenging conditions.

faqs.question: Is this trail suitable for beginners or families?
faqs.answer: No, this is a difficult hike requiring very good fitness and some alpine experience. It involves a significant elevation gain over 15 km, with steep ascents, unstable terrain, and some scrambling. It's not recommended for beginners or young children. Sure-footedness and sturdy shoes are essential.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trailhead?
faqs.answer: The circular route often starts from locations such as the Morana Electric Plant or the village of Purujosa. You'll typically find parking available near these starting points, which serve as common access points for exploring the Moncayo Natural Park.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this trail?
faqs.answer: Generally, dogs are allowed on trails within the Moncayo Natural Park, but it's always best to keep them on a leash to protect local wildlife and ensure their safety, especially given the challenging terrain and potential for steep sections.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or is there an entrance fee for the Moncayo Natural Park?
faqs.answer: There are typically no specific permits required or entrance fees to hike this trail within the Moncayo Natural Park. However, always respect park regulations, stay on marked paths where possible, and practice Leave No Trace principles.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain should I expect on the hike?
faqs.answer: The trail features a diverse mix of unpaved and gravel paths. You'll traverse the Horcajuelo ravine, known for its high humidity and varied vegetation, including abundant hollies. Expect rocky formations, some sections with unstable scree ('canchal y pedrizas'), and parts that may require an ascent through a 'canal' to reach higher ground. Some sections might also be un-marked.

faqs.question: What are the main highlights and viewpoints along the route?
faqs.answer: Key highlights include the unique Cueva de los Pilares, an open cave with impressive natural pillars. You'll also ascend to the Cerro Morrón Summit, offering extensive panoramic views of the eastern side of Moncayo and the Peñas de Herrera. The Horcajuelo ravine itself is a highlight with its distinct microclimate and rich plant life.

faqs.question: Does this trail intersect with other hiking routes?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route briefly intersects with sections of other official trails. You'll find it overlaps with parts of 'Etapa 2: Santuario del Moncayo - Purujosa', 'Ruta 1 Cerro Gordo', and 'Etapa 1: Collado de la Estaca - Talamantes'.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this challenging hike?
faqs.answer: Given the difficult nature of this trail, you should bring sturdy hiking boots with good ankle support, plenty of water, snacks, appropriate layers of clothing for changing weather, a map and compass or GPS device (as some sections can be un-marked), and sun protection. Trekking poles can also be very helpful on steep or unstable sections.

faqs.question: Are there any facilities like cafes or restaurants near the trailhead?
faqs.answer: The trail often starts from villages like Purujosa or near the Morana Electric Plant. While these are not major tourist hubs, you might find basic amenities, small cafes, or local restaurants in Purujosa or other nearby villages within the Moncayo Natural Park, but it's advisable to check opening hours in advance, especially outside peak season.

faqs.question: What makes the Cueva de los Pilares unique?
faqs.answer: The Cueva de los Pilares is unique because it's largely open to the exterior, meaning you don't typically need a headlamp for exploration. Inside, several natural 'pillars' have formed, creating an impressive, vault-like structure that is quite spectacular to witness.

waypoints.name: Views of the Peñas de Herrera
